en.extract:
The amethyst sunbird, also called the black sunbird (Chalcomitra amethystina), is a species of passerine bird in the family Nectariniidae. It is native to the Afrotropics, mostly south of the equator. They are commonly found in well-watered habitats, and undertake seasonal movements to visit flowering woodlands. The demise of some woodlands have impacted their numbers locally, but their range has also expanded along with the spread of wooded gardens.


Habits
Though mostly found singly or in pairs, larger numbers may concentrate at favourite flowering trees, where they act aggressively towards other sunbird species. The complex song is a loud, sustained twittering. Food includes emergent termites, spiders and nectar. In courtship a male will hop about a branch near a female, drop one wing, then the other, and finally both wings. The wings will then be fluttered and displayed. A responsive female may lower her head, and assume a rigid posture.


Nesting
Breeding pairs of amethyst sunbird are widely spaced, and the female builds the nest. Favoured trees include exotic eucalypts and pines, and are often close to buildings or human activity. Nests are attached to a drooping branch, below the canopy, or hidden by foliage. Nests are built from fine grass stems, which are bound together with cobwebs. The nest is often decorated with lichens, or other debris. Two speckled eggs are laid, but successive clutches may be raised from the same nest in a single season. Nests are parasitized by green-backed honeybird and Klaas's cuckoo.


Habitat and movements
Amethyst sunbirds are widespread residents of woodland, mesic savanna, forest edge and suburban gardens. They occur only sparsely in dry savanna or low dry regions, where they keep to riparian woods or concentrations of nectar-bearing plants, and are decidedly scarce in the Limpopo valley and mopane regions. A distinct summer influx is notable in the Zambezi valley and Great Zimbabwe woodlands, and they are strictly summer visitors (September/October to April) to the Kalahari sand (or Gusu) woodlands, where they appear in high densities when the Baikiaea trees are in flower. On seaward-facing slopes, they are very common residents up to 1,800 metres, with high reporting rates in afromontane forest and valley bushveld.


Adaptations
The average hematocrit level for birds is between 30 and 45%; however, the Amethyst Sunbird has an average hematocrit level of 50%. Amethyst sunbirds can be found along an altitudinal gradient in South Africa from the Drakensberg mountain range to the coast of KwaZulu-Natal. During warmer months, the sunbird's hematocrit levels will increase with higher altitudes (where there is also a dramatic drop in temperature) and with lower oxygen concentrations in the atmosphere.  Higher hematocrit levels may be attributed to its small body size and can help with oxygen carrying capacity and the metabolic cost of flying.
Subpopulations of Amethyst Sunbirds show variation in their resting metabolic rates, basal metabolic rate, and thermal neutral zones based on the subpopulation's acclimation to different altitudes. Those more acclimated to high altitudes would have lower metabolic rates and a larger thermal neutral zone. Therefore, the Amethyst Sunbird can change its physiology in response to altitude and temperature to achieve a metabolic rate and temperature regulation best suited for its environment.


Status
On the Mashonaland plateau, race kirkii has declined in favour of scarlet-chested sunbird, after fragmentation of its native miombo woodlands. In South Africa, the range of the nominate race has increased along with the spread of wooded gardens.


Range
It occurs in Angola, Botswana, Burundi, Republic of the Congo, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Eswatini, Ethiopia, Gabon, Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, Namibia, Somalia, South Africa, South Sudan, Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia and Zimbabwe.


Races
Between three and eleven races have been proposed, including:

C. a. amethystina
Range: South Africa, southern Botswana, Eswatini
Description: Fairly distinct: bill heavy, females dark from throat to belly, males have tail coverts metallic purple
C. a. kirkii (Shelley, 1876)
Range: Zimbabwe to East Africa
Description: Bill finer than nominate, females much lighter on underside, males with dark brown (not purple) tail coverts
C. a. kalckreuthi (Cabanis, 1878)
Range: coastal Tanzania and Kenya
Description: Bill slender, females pale below, unmarked on throat, supercilium long, males may have metallic purple upper tail coverts; often merged with C. a. kirkii
C. a. deminuta  Cabanis, 1880
Range: West African tropics
C. a. doggetti (Sharpe, 1902)
Range: western Kenya
C. a. adjuncta (Clancey, 1975)
Range: Eswatini
Description: Similar to nominate, with which it is often merged


Gallery


References


External links

(Black Sunbird =) Amethyst Sunbird - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.

The '''amethyst sunbird''', also called the '''black sunbird''' ('''''Chalcomitra amethystina'''''), is a [[species]] of [[passerine]] [[bird]] in the family [[Nectariniidae]]. It is native to the [[Afrotropical realm|Afrotropics]], mostly south of the equator.<ref name=iziko>{{cite web |title = Chalcomitra amethystina (Amethyst sunbird, Black sunbird) | url = http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/nectariniidae/chalcomitra_amethystina.htm |website=biodiversityexplorer.org |publisher=iziko museums |access-date=15 October 2014}}</ref> They are commonly found in well-watered [[habitat]]s, and undertake seasonal movements to visit flowering woodlands. The demise of some woodlands have impacted their numbers locally, but their range has also expanded along with the spread of wooded gardens.

==Habits==
Though mostly found singly or in pairs, larger numbers may concentrate at favourite flowering trees, where they act aggressively towards other sunbird species.<ref name=Zimmer>{{cite book |last1=Zimmerman |first1=Dale A. |title=Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania |date=1996 |publisher=Russel Friedman Books |location=Halfway House |isbn=1-875091-04-1 |page=642|display-authors=etal}}</ref> The complex song is a loud, sustained twittering.<ref name=Zimmer/> Food includes emergent termites, spiders and nectar.<ref name=hbw>{{cite web |title=Amethyst Sunbird (Chalcomitra amethystina) |url=http://www.hbw.com/species/amethyst-sunbird-chalcomitra-amethystina |website=hbw.com |publisher=Handbook of the Birds of the World|access-date=15 October 2014}}</ref> In courtship a male will hop about a branch near a female, drop one wing, then the other, and finally both wings. The wings will then be fluttered and displayed. A responsive female may lower her head, and assume a rigid posture.<ref name=auk>{{cite journal |last1=Cowles |first1=Raymond B. |title=Observations on the Amethyst Sunbird, Chalcomitra amethystina amethystina (Shaw) |journal=The Auk |date=January 1936 |volume=53 |issue=1 |pages=28–30 |doi=10.2307/4077353 |jstor=4077353 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

==Nesting==
Breeding pairs of amethyst sunbird are widely spaced, and the female builds the nest.<ref name="tarb">{{cite book |last1=Tarboton |first1=Warwick |title=Field guide to nests & eggs of Southern African birds |date=2000 |publisher=Struik |location=Cape Town |isbn=1-86872-271-6|pages=235–236}}</ref> Favoured trees include exotic eucalypts and pines, and are often close to buildings or human activity.<ref name="auk" /> Nests are attached to a drooping branch, ''below'' the canopy, or hidden by foliage.<ref name="tarb" /> Nests are built from fine grass stems, which are bound together with cobwebs.<ref name="tarb" /> The nest is often decorated with lichens,<ref name="Zimmer" /> or other debris. Two speckled eggs are laid, but successive clutches may be raised from the same nest in a single season.<ref name="tarb" /> Nests are parasitized by [[green-backed honeybird]] and [[Klaas's cuckoo]].<ref name="iziko" />

==Habitat and movements==
Amethyst sunbirds are widespread residents of woodland, mesic [[savanna]], forest edge and suburban gardens.<ref name=Zimmer/> They occur only sparsely in dry [[savanna]]<ref name=irw>{{cite book |last1=Irwin |first1=Michael P. Stuart |title=The birds of Zimbabwe |date=1981 |publisher=Quest Pub. |location=Salisbury, Zimbabwe |isbn=0-86925-156-2|pages=375–376}}</ref> or low dry regions, where they keep to riparian woods or concentrations of nectar-bearing plants,<ref name=Zimmer/><ref name=irw/> and are decidedly scarce in the [[Limpopo river|Limpopo]] valley and [[mopane]] regions.<ref name=sabap/> A distinct summer influx is notable in the [[Zambezi river|Zambezi]] valley and [[Great Zimbabwe]] woodlands,<ref name=irw/> and they are strictly summer visitors (September/October to April) to the Kalahari sand (or Gusu) woodlands, where they appear in high densities when the [[Baikiaea plurijuga|''Baikiaea'' trees]] are in flower.<ref name=irw/> On seaward-facing slopes, they are very common residents up to 1,800 metres,<ref name=irw/> with high reporting rates in [[afromontane]] forest and [[Maputaland-Pondoland bushland and thickets|valley bushveld]].<ref name=sabap>{{cite book|last=Harisson |first=J.A. |title=The atlas of southern African birds |year=1997 |publisher=BirdLife SA |location=Johannesburg |isbn=0-620-20730-2 | url=http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/792.pdf |pages=518–520|display-authors=etal}}</ref>

==Adaptations==
The average [[hematocrit]] level for birds is between 30 and 45%; however, the Amethyst Sunbird has an average hematocrit level of 50%.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Marsen|first=Sky|date=2009-02-01|title=Book review: BARBARA JOHNSTONE, Discourse Analysis, 2nd edn. Oxford: Blackwell, 2008. 312 pp|url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/1461445608101544|journal=Discourse Studies|language=en|volume=11|issue=1|pages=121–123|doi=10.1177/1461445608101544|s2cid=143397992 |issn=1461-4456|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Amethyst sunbirds can be found along an altitudinal gradient in South Africa from the Drakensberg mountain range to the coast of KwaZulu-Natal.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yom-Tov|first=Yoram|date=2002-09-01|title=Sunbirds: A Guide to the Sunbirds, Flowerpeckers, Spiderhunters, and Sugarbirds of the World. By Robert A Cheke and Clive F Mann; illustrated by Richard Allen|url=https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1086/345222|journal=The Quarterly Review of Biology|volume=77|issue=3|pages=340–341|doi=10.1086/345222|issn=0033-5770|url-access=subscription}}</ref> During warmer months, the sunbird's hematocrit levels will increase with higher altitudes (where there is also a dramatic drop in temperature) and with lower oxygen concentrations in the atmosphere.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Lindsay|first1=Claire V.|last2=Downs|first2=Colleen T.|last3=Brown|first3=Mark|date=2010-10-01|title=Seasonal variation in hematocrit levels of Amethyst Sunbirds (Chalcomitra amethystina) over an altitudinal gradient|journal=Journal of Ornithology|language=en|volume=151|issue=4|pages=851–855|doi=10.1007/s10336-010-0522-z|s2cid=37418670 |issn=1439-0361}}</ref>  Higher hematocrit levels may be attributed to its small body size and can help with oxygen carrying capacity and the metabolic cost of flying.<ref name=":0" />

Subpopulations of Amethyst Sunbirds show variation in their [[resting metabolic rate]]s, [[basal metabolic rate]], and [[thermal neutral zone]]s based on the subpopulation's acclimation to different altitudes.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Lindsay|first1=Claire V.|last2=Downs|first2=Colleen T.|last3=Brown|first3=Mark|date=2009-02-15|title=Physiological variation in amethyst sunbirds (Chalcomitra amethystina) over an altitudinal gradient in winter|url=https://jeb.biologists.org/content/212/4/483|journal=Journal of Experimental Biology|language=en|volume=212|issue=4|pages=483–493|doi=10.1242/jeb.025262|issn=0022-0949|pmid=19181895|doi-access=free|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Those more acclimated to high altitudes would have lower metabolic rates and a larger thermal neutral zone. Therefore, the Amethyst Sunbird can change its physiology in response to altitude and temperature to achieve a metabolic rate and temperature regulation best suited for its environment.<ref name=":1" />

==Status==
On the [[Mashonaland]] plateau, race ''kirkii'' has declined in favour of [[scarlet-chested sunbird]], after fragmentation of its native [[miombo]] woodlands.<ref name=sabap/> In [[South Africa]], the range of the nominate race has increased along with the spread of wooded gardens.<ref name=iziko/>

==Range==
It occurs in [[Angola]], [[Botswana]], [[Burundi]], [[Republic of the Congo]], [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]], [[Eswatini]], [[Ethiopia]], [[Gabon]], [[Kenya]], [[Malawi]], [[Mozambique]], [[Namibia]], [[Somalia]], [[South Africa]], [[South Sudan]], [[Tanzania]], [[Uganda]], [[Zambia]] and [[Zimbabwe]].

==Races==
Between three<ref name=ibc>{{cite web|title=Amethyst Sunbird (Chalcomitra amethystina) |url=http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/amethyst-sunbird-chalcomitra-amethystina |website=The Internet Bird Collection |publisher=IBC |access-date=15 October 2014}}</ref> and eleven<ref name=geovar>{{cite book |last1=Chittenden |first1=Hugh |last2=Allan |first2=David |last3=Weiersbye |first3=Ingrid (art) |title=Roberts geographic variation of southern African birds |date=2012 |publisher=John Voelcker Bird Book Fund |location=Cape Town |isbn=978-1-920602-00-0}}</ref> races have been proposed, including:
* ''C. a. amethystina'' 
::Range: South Africa, southern Botswana, Eswatini
::Description: Fairly distinct: bill heavy, females dark from throat to belly, males have tail coverts metallic purple
* ''C. a. kirkii'' <small>(Shelley, 1876)</small>
::Range: Zimbabwe to East Africa
::Description: Bill finer than nominate, females much lighter on underside, males with dark brown (not purple) tail coverts
* ''C. a. kalckreuthi'' <small>(Cabanis, 1878)</small>
::Range: coastal Tanzania and Kenya
::Description: Bill slender, females pale below, unmarked on throat, supercilium long, males may have metallic purple upper tail coverts;<ref name=Zimmer/> often merged with ''C. a. kirkii''
* ''C. a. deminuta '' <small>Cabanis, 1880</small>
::Range: West African tropics
* ''C. a. doggetti'' <small>(Sharpe, 1902)</small>
::Range: western Kenya
* ''C. a. adjuncta'' <small>(Clancey, 1975)</small>
::Range: Eswatini
::Description: Similar to nominate, with which it is often merged
